qualification_assessment.qualification_basis: Multiple terms carry explicit inner-being location statements: odune (pain in the heart, Rom 9:2; pangs piercing through, 1Tim 6:10), sunoche (anguish of heart, 2Cor 2:4), chul (anguish in the heart, Psa 55:4), mar (bitterness of soul, Job 3:20; 7:11; 10:1; 21:25; Isa 38:15), ya.gon (sorrow in the heart, Psa 13:2), qo.tser (shortness of spirit, Exo 6:9), tsa.rah (distress of soul, Gen 42:21; Psa 31:7; Jon 2:2). The term set includes the widest kinetic-somatic anguish vocabulary in the 181 series so far: the chul root names anguish as twisting and writhing of the body, with the travail of childbirth as its primary physical image. The qualification is moderately confident: a significant portion of the verse set covers external-circumstantial distress (ra.ah/disaster, enemy-register tsar uses) with limited direct inner-being content.
qualification_assessment.qualification_flag: Source list is Missing Inner Being Words, indicating anguish was not in the original Framework B inner-being list. The qualification depends substantially on locating the inner-being sub-sense within terms that also carry situational and external-distress meanings. Two originally proposed core terms (stenochoria and tsar/tsa.rah) are predominantly situational. stenochoria has no term block in the source file (MISSING_TERM flag). ra.ah (684 occurrences) and tsar-enemy (69 occurrences) are flagged as PERIPHERAL.

term_inventory.meaning_summary: Anguish, grief, pain of body or mind; sorrow. LSJ: pain of body; pain of mind, grief, distress. Related: odunaō (be anguished, G3600).

term_inventory.anchor_grammatical_form: Verb Present Middle Participle Nominative Plural Masculine
term_inventory.meaning_summary: To grieve, be anxious, be in agony; to pain bodily or mentally; passive: to be in agony, be tormented, be distressed. LSJ: cause pain or suffering; passive: feel pain, suffer pain.

Anguish, distress; primarily a being held together, compression; in NT metaphorically distress of mind, anxiety. LSJ: holding together; contraction at a narrow point of road or sea; conflict in battle; continuity; metaphorically distress, affliction, oppression, imprisonment. Root verb: sunechō (to hold/oppress).

Contest, violent struggle; agony, anguish, anxiety. LSJ: contest and struggle for victory; gymnastic exercise; of the mind — agony and anguish in fear and great distress. Related: agōn (fight), agōnizomai (to struggle), antagōnizomai, epagōnizomai, katagōnizomai, sunagōnizomai.

Trouble, distress, oppression, tribulation, pressure; primarily physical pressure or compression; metaphorically affliction, distress of mind (2Cor 2:4), distressing circumstances, trial, affliction. LSJ: pressure; crushing; metaphorically oppression and affliction. Related: apothlibō (crowd up to), thlibō (press on), sunthlibō (push against).

Pain, agony; hard work, toil, travail; labour and pain; pain, misery, anguish, agony. LSJ: toil especially of war; stress, trouble, distress, suffering; physical pain; things produced by work. Personified as son of Eris in classical literature. Related: penēs (poor).

term_inventory.anchor_grammatical_form: Verb Present Passive Participle Nominative Singular Masculine
term_inventory.meaning_summary: To torture, torment; passive: to be tortured, tormented, in pain; primarily to apply the touchstone; metaphorically to examine, scrutinize, try; in NT to afflict, torment; passive: to be afflicted, tormented, pained by diseases or tossed by waves. LSJ: rub on touchstone; put to the test; question by torture. Related: basanismos (torment), basanistēs (torturer), basanos (torment).

Bitter, bitterness — of water or food; of the end of wickedness; of pain as substantive; bitterly as adverb. Related: ma.rar (to make bitter/provoke), me.ri.rut/mo.rah/mor.rah/mam.ror/tam.rur (bitterness variants), me.re.rah/me.ro.rah (gall), mor (myrrh), ma.ror (bitterness).
Straits, distress, trouble. Root cluster: ya.tsar (be distressed), me.tsar (terror/straits), tsar (narrow/distress/enemy/hard), tse.ror (bundle/pebble), tsa.rar (to constrain/distress/vex/rival).
Straits, distress. Shares root with tsa.rah (distress H6869B) and tsar (enemy H6862C). The distress and enemy senses are separate STEP sub-entries of the same root lemma.

Straitness, straits, distress, stress, anguish. Root cluster: mu.tsaq (constraint), tsuq (to press), tsoq (distress), tsu.qah (anguish), me.tsu.qah (distress) — all related words with no independent blocks in the source file.
Constraint, distress. Root: ya.tsaq (to pour, cast metal, set down, be firm). Related: ma.tsoq, me.tsu.qah, tsuq, tsoq, tsu.qah (all root-cluster members, no independent blocks).
Straits, distress. Note: STEP heading gloss is terror but the meaning section gives straits/distress. Root-related to tsar/tsa.rah cluster.

To twist, whirl, dance, writhe, fear, tremble, travail, be in anguish, be pained. Full stem range — Qal: dance, twist, writhe, whirl; Polel: dance, writhe in travail/bear/bring forth, wait anxiously; Pulal: be made to writhe, be brought forth; Hophal: be born; Hithpolel: whirling, writhing/suffering torture, wait longingly; Hithpalpel: be distressed. The root encompasses the full kinetic range of involuntary bodily movement from dance to birthpang to terror-trembling. STEP divides into five sub-entries (H2342A tremble, H2342H give birth, H2342I writhe in pain, H2342J dance, H2342K anticipate) — consolidated here. Note: chil H2342B (be firm/strong) is a different lemma, not consolidated.

Pain, agony, sorrow; a writhing, anguish; writhing of fear; anguish. Also chi.lah (H2427B). Derived from chul root. Related cluster: chal.cha.lah (anguish H2479), chol (sand), chel (rampart), cha.yil (strength).
Pain, trembling, terror, writhing, anguish. Reduplicated form intensifying the chul twisting root semantically. Related cluster: same as chil above.
Cramp, agony, anguish — meaning uncertain. Related: mish.be.tsot (filigree), sha.vats (to weave), tash.bets (checkered). Root connection to weaving/filigree suggests cramping or tight-binding sense.
Shuddering, anguish. Related: qa.phad (to roll), qip.pod (porcupine). Root image: rolling/curling-inward like a porcupine in defence — a shuddering-contraction sense.
Grief, sorrow, anguish. Related: ya.gah (to suffer), tu.gah (grief).

Shortness, impatience, anguish. Related: qa.tser (be short/short), qa.tsar (to reap). Single occurrence: Exo 6:9 — qo.tser ru.ach (shortness of spirit) — anguish as contraction/crushing of the spirit.
Growling, groaning. Related: na.ham (to groan), na.ham (roaring). Single inner-being verse: Psa 38:8 — I groan because of the tumult of my heart.

Pain, sorrow, travail, pang; specifically pains of travail and birth pangs. Related: cha.val (to pledge, to destroy, to be in labour), cho.vel (pilot), tach.bu.lah (counsel), cord/destruction/pledge forms.
Pang, distress. Related: tsur (to confine, provoke, form), tsir (envoy, hinge, image), tsa.yar (to take provision). Pang/distress and envoy/hinge are separate sub-entries of the same root.

term_inventory.anchor_grammatical_form: adjective common singular either gender absolute
term_inventory.meaning_summary: To pain, grieve; P'al (Aramaic stem): pained as participle. Related cluster: ma.a.tse.vah (torment), a.tsav (to shape), e.tsev (toil/vessel), o.tsev (pain/idol), a.tsev (worker), its.tsa.von (toil), ats.tse.vet (injury). A carving-and-pain root cluster.
Excitement, anguish — of terror. Related: ur (to rouse), ir (city — large separate entry H5892). Thin-data sub-entry sharing lemma space with the city entry.

term_inventory.anchor_grammatical_form: noun common singular either gender absolute
term_inventory.meaning_summary: Toil, trouble, labour — trouble; trouble and mischief; toil and labour. Related: a.mal (to toil), a.mel (labourer). Predominantly Ecclesiastes toil vocabulary; the anguish sub-sense appears in Isa 53:11 (out of the anguish of his soul).

Evil, misery, distress, injury — evil/misery/distress; evil/injury/wrong; evil (ethical). Overwhelmingly an evil/harm/disaster term. Related: me.ra (mischief), ra.a (to be evil/shatter). PERIPHERAL for inner-being analysis.
Adversary, foe, enemy, oppressor. Shares root with distress-tsar (H6862B) and tsa.rah. Listed in source term index but carries no inner-being anguish content. PERIPHERAL.

odune.reason: unceasing anguish in my heart — explicit inner-being location
pangs piercing those who crave money — inner state consequence of a misdirected desire

oduna\u014d.reason: searching for Jesus in great distress — parental inner anguish
I am in anguish in this flame — eschatological/parabolic
you are in anguish — parabolic continuation
sorrowful because of the word — inner grief at relational separation

sunoche.reason: anguish of heart and many tears — explicit inner-being location; Paul's pastoral inner state

ag\u014dnia.reason: being in agony he prayed more earnestly — explicit inner state in Gethsemane; somatic expression in bloody sweat

thlipsis.reason: much affliction and anguish of heart — inner-being location
utterly burdened beyond strength, despaired of life itself — severe inner pressure
woman no longer remembers the anguish for joy — inner-being state overtaken by joy
tribulation listed among things that cannot separate from love of Christ
through many tribulations we enter the kingdom
we are destined for afflictions — apostolic teaching
tribulation and distress for every evildoer — judicial/ethical

ponos.reason: gnawed tongues in anguish — eschatological judgment
cursed God for their pain — eschatological
no more pain — eschatological reversal naming the present inner state

basaniz\u014d.reason: boat beaten by waves — physical metaphor

mar.reason: bitter in soul — explicit soul location
anguish of my spirit and bitterness of my soul — spirit and soul both named
bitterness of my soul — explicit soul location
bitterness of my soul — Hezekiah facing death
for my welfare I had great bitterness — inner state with theological reversal
deeply distressed, wept bitterly — Hannah's inner-being grief
bitterness in the heat of my spirit — prophetic inner state; spirit location
bitter as wormwood — wisdom metaphor for immoral woman's end

tsa.rah.reason: distress of his soul — explicit soul location; brothers' recognition of Joseph's inner anguish
you have known the distress of my soul — explicit soul location in prayer
out of my distress I cried — inner state in extremis
my soul refuses to be comforted — inner state of ongoing distress
troubles of my heart are enlarged — inner location
time of distress for Jacob — national/eschatological
time of trouble such as never was — eschatological

chul.reason: my heart is in anguish within me — explicit inner-being heart location
struck them but they felt no anguish — inner-being response (or non-response) to discipline
we writhed but gave birth to wind — metaphor of inner effort producing nothing
writhe and groan O daughter of Zion — national anguish using inner-being physical image
they will be in anguish over the report — national distress

chil.reason: pangs seized inhabitants of Philistia — national terror/writhing
anguish as of a woman in labor — national register
pain seized you like a woman in labor — national anguish

chal.cha.lah.reason: anguish shall be in Cush — national/prophetic judgment
anguish shall come upon them — national
anguish in all loins — somatic national image

sha.vats.reason: anguish has seized me and yet my life still lingers — inner-being state in extremis; Saul's dying soldier begging to die

qe.pha.dah.reason: when anguish comes they will seek peace — national judgment context

ya.gon.reason: sorrow in my heart all the day — explicit inner location
my life is spent with sorrow — chronic inner state
I suffered distress and anguish — inner-being near-death experience
grief is upon me; my heart is sick within me — inner location
Lord has added sorrow to my pain — accumulated inner-being states (Baruch's complaint)
bring down my gray hairs with sorrow to Sheol — deep inner grief as life-trajectory
sorrow and sighing shall flee away — eschatological reversal

qo.tser.reason: they did not listen because of shortness of spirit (qo.tser ru.ach) — explicit spirit location; anguish as crushing/contraction of the spirit under slavery

ne.ha.mah.reason: I groan because of the tumult of my heart — explicit heart location

che.vel.reason: pangs and agony will seize them — eschatological/national
anguish and sorrows have taken hold of her as of a woman in labor — national register
pangs of Sheol laid hold on me — existential inner anguish

tsir.reason: my loins are filled with anguish — explicit bodily location; Isaiah's prophetic inner anguish
by reason of the vision pains have come upon me and I retain no strength — inner anguish from visionary experience

a.tsav.reason: cried out in a tone of anguish — Darius's inner anguish audible in his voice

a.mal.reason: out of the anguish of his soul he shall see — explicit soul location; the Servant's vicarious inner suffering

ra.ah.reason: calamities and distresses — situational disaster listing

tsar (enemy).reason: All 69 verses describe external adversaries/enemies; no inner-being anguish content

cross_registry_links.connecting_term: ya.gon (H3015) — grief, sorrow, anguish; tu.gah (grief) in the same root cluster
cross_registry_links.note: ya.gon spans grief and anguish with significant overlap. If grief is a separate entry in the 181, shared terms and verses will require boundary delineation in Phase 2.

cross_registry_links.connecting_term: mar (H4751) — bitter/bitterness; the bitterness-of-soul idiom appears in multiple inner-being lament contexts
cross_registry_links.note: mar covers physical-taste bitterness, inner-being bitterness-of-soul, and wisdom-metaphor bitterness. If bitterness is a separate entry in the 181, mar will need allocation between the two entries.
thlipsis covers both inner-being distress (anguish of heart, 2Cor 2:4) and external tribulation. If tribulation or suffering is a separate entry, thlipsis may need splitting.
The most explicit OT spirit-level anguish statement: anguish as contraction/crushing of the spirit. Connects the anguish entry directly to the ruach/spirit entry in the 181.

cross_registry_links.connecting_term: agōnia (G0074), chil (H2427A), chal.cha.lah (H2479) — shared with the previously completed agony entry
cross_registry_links.note: The agony entry (Phase 1 complete) shares multiple terms with anguish. The boundary between agony and anguish in the 181 requires Phase 2 delineation: agony tends toward acute/violent intensity, anguish toward sustained/interior distress.
The source word search at line 20 explicitly groups distress and anguish together. If distress is a separate entry in the 181, the tsar/tsa.rah cluster and stenochōria will need allocation.

cross_registry_links.connecting_term: chul root (H2342) — writhing/giving birth/anguish in a single kinetic root
cross_registry_links.note: The chul root unifies anguish and the travail of childbirth. If travail is a separate entry in the 181, the chul sub-senses (give birth vs writhe in pain) will need careful allocation.

cross_registry_links.connecting_term: a.mal (H5999) in Isa 53:11; agōnia (G0074) in Luk 22:44
cross_registry_links.note: Two of the most theologically significant anguish verses in Scripture are the Servant's soul-anguish (Isa 53:11, a.mal of the soul) and Jesus's Gethsemane agōnia (Luk 22:44). These verses connect the anguish entry to the substitutionary atonement and incarnation themes central to Framework B's theological integration.

data_quality_flags.description: stenochōria (hardship/anguish/narrowness) is listed as a core proposed term in the source list introduction (line 14) and in the STEP term index (line 44), but has no independent term block in the source file. Rom 2:9 and 2Cor 6:4 appear in the thlipsis verse set but are not attributed to stenochōria independently. Requires independent STEP search to recover.
tsu.qah (anguish) appears in the STEP term index (line 30) and in related-words lists under mu.tsaq and ma.tsoq, but has no independent term block. Requires independent STEP search.
me.tsu.qah (distress) appears in the STEP term index (line 36) and in related-words lists, but has no independent term block. Requires independent STEP search.
tsuq (to press) and tsoq (distress) appear in related-words lists under the constraint/distress cluster but have no independent term blocks.

chul appears under five separate STEP sub-entry headings: H2342A (tremble), H2342H (give birth), H2342I (writhe in pain), H2342J (dance), H2342K (anticipate) — all with 56 occurrences and identical meaning sections. These are anchor-sense divisions of a single lexeme and are consolidated to one entry. Note: chil (H2342B — be firm/strong, 2 occurrences) is a distinct lemma and is treated separately.

ra.ah (evil/misery/distress/injury) has 684 occurrences and is overwhelmingly an evil/harm/disaster term. Its inner-being anguish sub-sense is minimal. Flagged as PERIPHERAL for Framework B inner-being analysis.
tsar in the adversary/enemy sense has 69 occurrences but carries no inner-being anguish content. It shares root with distress-tsar (H6862B) and tsa.rah. Flagged as PERIPHERAL.
The ponos term block heading at line 1571 lacks the triple-hash header marker (###) present on all other term blocks. Data is otherwise complete.
